user_content: The coastal trip was amazing! One of my favorite shots from the trip is a portrait of my friend standing on a cliff overlooking the ocean. I used a fast shutter speed to freeze the motion of the waves and the wind blowing through her hair. The 50mm f/1.8 lens really brought out the details in her face and the background, and the shallow depth of field helped to separate her from the scenery. I'm thinking of printing that one out and framing it for my living room.

I've been playing around with the lens, trying to get used to the manual focus ring. It's definitely taking some getting used to, but the image quality is so much better than my old kit lens. I took it with me on the coastal trip and got some great portraits of my friends.

I've been using Lightroom for most of my editing, and I'm pretty comfortable with it. I'm aiming for a natural look that still brings out the vibrant colors of the coast. Last Sunday, I went to the nearby park and took some photos of the flowers and trees, and I was experimenting with different apertures and shutter speeds to get some nice bokeh effects. But I think the coastal shots from the week before that are more striking - we spent three days driving along the coastal highway, stopping at all the scenic overlooks and taking in the views.
I'm looking for some tips on editing photos of landscapes. I just got back from a road trip to the coast with friends last week and took some amazing shots of the cliffs and beaches.

I've been trying to improve my photography skills and was wondering if you could give me some tips on how to take better portraits. I've been experimenting with different angles and lighting setups, and I recently got a new 50mm f/1.8 prime lens that I'm still getting used to.

I did have to make some adjustments to my camera settings to account for the bright coastal light. I had to dial down the exposure compensation to avoid blowing out the highlights in the sky, and I also used a polarizing filter to reduce the glare from the water. Additionally, I shot in RAW format to give myself more flexibility during post-processing.

As for printing and framing, I'm thinking of going with a traditional print and frame. I want the image to have a classic, timeless feel to it, and I think a traditional print and frame will help achieve that. I'm considering using a matte finish to reduce glare and add some texture to the image. I've also been looking at some different frame styles, maybe something with a bit of a rustic or coastal vibe to match the theme of the image.

By the way, speaking of post-processing, I've been meaning to learn more about Lightroom and Photoshop. Do you have any tips or resources you could recommend for getting started with those programs
I'm especially interested in mastering my 50mm lens that I got a month ago. Can you give me some tips on how to best use its shallow depth of field to create a nice bokeh effect?
One of my favorites is a shot of a seagull flying overhead, its wings spread wide. I used a fast shutter speed to freeze the motion, and it turned out really well.
